title: Why Small Models Matter
concept: Efficient CNNs matter because real devices like phones, cameras, and drones have strict limits on latency, battery, memory, and heat, so the best model is often the one that fits the hardware instead of the one with the biggest benchmark score.

exercises.prompt: Recap: DenseNet's big idea was that later layers can do what instead of relearning everything from scratch?

options.text: Reuse earlier features through direct connections
Delete all earlier features before every new block

exercises.explain: Exactly — DenseNet emphasized feature reuse. Now the question becomes: how do we make useful CNNs fit small hardware too?

cards.title: A smart doorbell cannot wait forever
cards.body: A cloud server can take its time compared with a phone or a smart doorbell. If a doorbell camera needs a second or two to decide whether someone is at the door, the user already feels lag. Small models matter because many CNNs must run right now, on the device itself.

cards.title: What makes a model 'small' or efficient?
cards.body: An efficient CNN is designed to deliver useful accuracy with lower cost: fewer parameters to store, fewer operations to compute, less memory traffic, and lower energy use. On edge devices, success is not just 'Can it classify?' but 'Can it classify fast enough, within the device's limits?'

cards.title: Packing for a hike, not moving houses
cards.body: A data-center GPU is like moving with a truck: you can bring heavy furniture. A phone is like hiking with a backpack: every extra item costs energy and space. A model that is fine in the truck may be miserable in the backpack.

cards.title: The same CNN feels different on different hardware
cards.body: A large CNN might classify an image well on a server, but on a phone it can cause visible lag, drain battery, and compete with the camera, screen, and other apps for memory. A slightly smaller model that answers in 30 ms may create a much better real product than a larger one that answers in 300 ms.

cards.title: Highest accuracy is not always the best choice
cards.body: It is tempting to treat the top benchmark score as the winner. But a model that is 1% more accurate and 10x slower may be the worse choice for on-device use. In practice, engineers care about accuracy under constraints: memory, latency, heat, battery, and network availability.

cards.title: Server-friendly vs. device-friendly thinking
cards.body: Both can be useful, but they optimize for different environments.

left.text:
More room for compute
Latency can be hidden more easily
Power and memory are less tight

right.text:
Must answer quickly
Battery + heat matter
Memory budget is strict

exercises.prompt: Why might a smaller CNN be better than a larger one for a phone app that labels objects in live camera video?

options.text: Because lower latency and lower energy use can matter more than squeezing out a tiny extra accuracy gain
Because phones cannot perform convolutions at all
Because benchmark accuracy becomes meaningless on phones
Because small models automatically have perfect accuracy

exercises.explain: Right — for live on-device use, responsiveness and efficiency are part of model quality.

exercises.prompt: True or false: a model that is best on a benchmark leaderboard is automatically the best choice for every hardware setting.
exercises.explain: False — hardware limits change what 'best' means.

exercises.sentence: On edge devices, model quality includes accuracy plus hardware ___ like latency, memory, and battery.
exercises.explain: Exactly — efficient models are judged in context, not in a vacuum.

exercises.prompt: Which deployment scenario most strongly pushes you toward an efficient CNN?

options.text: A wildlife camera running on battery in a remote forest
An offline spreadsheet stored as a PDF
A static company logo saved on a desktop

exercises.explain: Yes — battery-powered edge devices feel every extra operation.

exercises.prompt: A team is choosing between two CNNs for an on-device translation camera. Model A is slightly more accurate but slow and power-hungry. Model B is a bit less accurate but runs smoothly in real time on the phone. Which choice best fits the product goal, and why?

options.text: Model B, because on-device products must balance accuracy with latency, energy, and memory limits
Model A, because any model with the top score is always the correct production choice
Model A, because phones can borrow unlimited battery from the network
Neither, because CNNs cannot run on phones

exercises.explain: Exactly. Real products care about usable performance under hardware limits, not just abstract leaderboard wins. Next: one famous answer to this problem — SqueezeNet, which tries to keep AlexNet-level performance with far fewer parameters.
